title: Trauma and the Sensory Integration System: Sensory Processing Disorder and Trauma
excerpt: Explore how trauma impacts the sensory integration system, linking Sensory Processing Disorder and trauma responses for lasting healing.
body:
Understanding Trauma's Impact on the Sensory Integration System

Trauma is more than a psychological event; it is a profound bodily experience that rewires how the brain and body process sensory information. While many people know trauma through emotional flashbacks or anxiety, fewer understand its deep influence on the sensory integration system. Sensory Processing Disorder (SPD) is often overlooked as a trauma-related condition, yet trauma can profoundly disrupt how the brain interprets and regulates incoming sensory data.

Somatic researchers like Peter Levine and Bessel van der Kolk have emphasized the body’s role in trauma, showing that trauma is stored in the nervous system, not just in memory. This article breaks down the science behind trauma’s effect on sensory processing, why SPD and trauma are often intertwined, and what this means for self-directed healing.

What Is the Sensory Integration System?

The sensory integration system refers to the brain’s ability to receive, organize, and interpret sensory input from the environment and the body’s internal states. This system integrates signals from multiple senses: touch, proprioception (body position), vestibular (balance and movement), auditory, visual, olfactory, and gustatory senses.

Dr. A. Jean Ayres, the pioneer of sensory integration theory, described how effective sensory processing is critical for learning, emotional regulation, and motor coordination. When sensory signals are processed smoothly, the brain can respond adaptively to the environment. But when sensory integration is disrupted, it can trigger dysregulation, overwhelm, or shutdown.

Sensory Processing Disorder (SPD)

SPD is a condition where the brain cannot properly receive or respond to sensory stimuli. Individuals with SPD may be hypersensitive (over-responsive), hyposensitive (under-responsive), or have difficulty integrating multiple sensory inputs. Symptoms include extreme discomfort with certain textures, sounds, or movements, poor motor coordination, and difficulty with attention and emotional regulation.

SPD is often diagnosed in children but can persist or emerge in adults, particularly those with a history of trauma.

How Trauma Alters Sensory Processing

Trauma triggers a cascade of neurophysiological changes that affect the sensory integration system. The nervous system’s primary job is to keep us safe, and trauma can recalibrate it to interpret benign sensory input as threatening.

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory explains how trauma affects the autonomic nervous system, particularly the vagus nerve pathways that regulate social engagement and safety signals. When the nervous system is stuck in a defensive mode, sensory inputs that might be neutral become overwhelming or numbing.

Research by Bessel van der Kolk in The Body Keeps the Score highlights how trauma survivors often experience somatic symptoms, including heightened bodily sensations and sensory dysregulation. This is because trauma memory is stored in the sensory and emotional parts of the brain, not the narrative centers.

“Trauma is not just an event that took place sometime in the past; it is also the imprint left by that experience on mind, brain, and body.” — Bessel van der Kolk

Hyper- and Hypo-Sensitivity Explained

Hyper-sensitivity in trauma survivors manifests as an exaggerated response to sensory stimuli—loud noises feel unbearable, touch is perceived as painful, or certain smells trigger panic. This is the nervous system’s defense mechanism, primed to detect danger.

Conversely, hypo-sensitivity can appear as numbness or detachment from sensory input, a shutdown response to overwhelming stress. Both extremes reflect the nervous system’s attempt to regulate itself in the aftermath of trauma.

Why Trauma and SPD Are Frequently Linked

Many individuals with SPD have histories of early childhood trauma, neglect, or adverse experiences. Conversely, trauma can induce sensory processing difficulties even if SPD was not present before the traumatic event.

Dr. Pat Ogden, a leader in somatic psychology, emphasizes that trauma disrupts the brain’s ability to integrate sensory information, leading to symptoms commonly diagnosed as SPD. This means SPD symptoms may be a manifestation of unresolved trauma rather than a standalone neurological disorder.

Gabor Maté points out that early relational trauma affects the development of the nervous system, altering sensory thresholds and emotional regulation capacities. This creates a feedback loop where sensory dysregulation exacerbates trauma symptoms and vice versa.

Symptoms to Watch For: When Trauma Mimics or Causes SPD

  • Unusual sensitivity or aversion to touch, sounds, or visual stimuli
  • Difficulty calming down after sensory overload
  • Emotional dysregulation triggered by sensory input
  • Motor coordination problems or clumsiness
  • Chronic feeling of being on edge or hypervigilant
  • Periods of dissociation or numbness related to sensory stimuli

If you’ve experienced trauma and recognize these symptoms, it is crucial to consider how trauma affects your sensory processing rather than treating SPD as an isolated issue.

Somatic Approaches to Healing Sensory Dysregulation from Trauma

Traditional talk therapy often struggles to address sensory integration difficulties because these issues are encoded in the body’s implicit memory systems. Somatic approaches focus on re-regulating the nervous system through body awareness and sensory modulation.

Peter Levine’s Somatic Experiencing works by gently tracking bodily sensations and allowing the nervous system to renegotiate trauma responses through titrated sensory experiences.

Deb Dana, a clinical social worker and expert on Polyvagal Theory, recommends cultivating safety via sensory grounding techniques such as slow, deep breathing, safe touch, and mindful movement to restore the nervous system’s capacity to process sensory input without overwhelm.

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) therapy can also be helpful, as it addresses the parts of the self that are triggered by sensory input and trauma, fostering internal safety and integration.

Practical Strategies for Self-Directed Healing

  • Mindful Sensory Exploration: Practice noticing sensory input without judgment. Use grounding objects like textured fabrics or gentle aromatherapy to anchor your awareness.
  • Movement and Vestibular Stimulation: Activities like gentle rocking, swinging, or yoga can help recalibrate the vestibular system and promote regulation.
  • Regulated Breathing: Engage in slow, diaphragmatic breathing to activate the parasympathetic nervous system and reduce sensory overwhelm.
  • Safe Touch: Self-massage or weighted blankets can provide calming proprioceptive input.
  • Limit Overstimulation: Create sensory-friendly environments by controlling light, noise, and tactile inputs.

The Road Ahead: Integrating Sensory and Trauma Healing

Understanding the interplay between trauma and sensory processing opens new pathways for healing that honor the body’s wisdom. As Dan Siegel emphasizes, integration—the linking of different parts of the nervous system and mind—is key to recovery.

Trauma-informed care that includes sensory integration awareness can reduce the sense of being stuck in trauma responses. Instead of pushing through confusion or overwhelm, you learn to listen to your body’s signals and respond with compassion and regulation.

Healing sensory dysregulation is not about “fixing” the body but about creating a safe internal environment where the nervous system can reorganize and reclaim balance.

“Healing doesn’t mean the damage never existed. It means the damage no longer controls our lives.” — Unknown

References

  • van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma. Penguin Books.
  • Levine, P. A. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice: How the Body Releases Trauma and Restores Goodness. North Atlantic Books.
  • Porges, S. W. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory: Neurophysiological Foundations of Emotions, Attachment, Communication, and Self-Regulation. W. W. Norton & Company.
  • Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy: Engaging the Rhythm of Regulation. W. W. Norton & Company.
  • Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body: A Sensorimotor Approach to Psychotherapy. W. W. Norton & Company.
  • Maté, G. (2018). In the Realm of Hungry Ghosts: Close Encounters with Addiction. North Atlantic Books.
  • Siegel, D. J. (2012). The Developing Mind: How Relationships and the Brain Interact to Shape Who We Are. Guilford Press.
  • Schwartz, R. C. (1995). Internal Family Systems Therapy. Guilford Press.

faqs.q: Can trauma cause Sensory Processing Disorder?
faqs.a: Yes, trauma can disrupt the nervous system's ability to process sensory information, leading to symptoms similar to Sensory Processing Disorder. Often SPD symptoms in trauma survivors reflect nervous system dysregulation rather than a primary sensory disorder.

faqs.q: How does Polyvagal Theory explain sensory sensitivity after trauma?
faqs.a: Polyvagal Theory shows how trauma can lock the nervous system into defensive states, making neutral sensory stimuli feel threatening. This leads to hyper- or hypo-sensitivity as the body tries to protect itself.

faqs.q: What somatic therapies help with trauma-related sensory issues?
faqs.a: Somatic Experiencing, Polyvagal-informed therapy, and Internal Family Systems are effective approaches. They focus on nervous system regulation, body awareness, and creating internal safety with sensory grounding.

faqs.q: Can self-directed healing improve sensory integration after trauma?
faqs.a: Yes, mindful sensory exploration, regulated breathing, movement, and creating sensory-friendly environments can help retrain the nervous system. Self-directed work supports nervous system regulation and reduces overwhelm.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book explaining how trauma affects the body and nervous system, essential for understanding sensory integration in trauma recovery.

metaDescription: Explore how trauma impacts sensory integration and Sensory Processing Disorder for deeper healing of body and mind.
